quote: just reaching back out here about that solar evaluation for your home. Looks like we did not get a chance to meet with you to go over that solar evaluation, but, I would love to reschedule that appointment.
stewart_read: Brandon's opener is purely logistical — reschedule the missed sit. There is no re-anchor of Sean's original motivation for signing up. On a reschedule call, the rep's first job is to remind the customer why they were interested in the first place before asking for a new time. Brandon skips that entirely and goes straight to calendar coordination. Strategic read: possibly Brandon is keeping it brief to avoid re-opening objections. Reactive read: Brandon simply defaulted to the administrative task. The reactive read holds — there is no evidence of a deliberate strategy to defer re-anchoring.
alternative_hypothesis: Brandon may have intentionally kept the opener brief to avoid re-opening objections on a warm reschedule.
coaching_implication: On reschedule calls, the opener should briefly re-anchor why Sean originally signed up — whatever that was — before asking for a new time. The manager should ask Brandon: 'What did you know about Sean's original motivation, and why didn't you use it here?' This is the curiosity-creation move applied to a reschedule context.

quote: Yeah. I forgot all about that shit yesterday.
stewart_read: Sean's first line is a reliability signal, not just an apology. A rep reading this correctly would recognize that the button-up protocol — verbal handshake, one-hour block, computer/no-driving anchor — is especially critical here. Brandon responds with 'That's alright,' which is socially smooth but strategically inert. The forgetfulness signal is absorbed, not converted.
coaching_implication: Show Brandon the button_up schema section. Ask him: 'Sean told you he forgets things. What did you do differently in the confirmation because of that?' The answer should be: verbal handshake, one-hour block stated, computer anchor. None of those appeared. This is the direct cause of the fragile-sit risk.

quote: Yeah. You sure you won't forget?
stewart_read: Brandon's line is the right instinct — he recognizes the reliability gap — but the execution is a mild jab rather than a structural fix. 'You sure you won't forget?' puts the burden on Sean to reassure Brandon, which Sean cannot do (his calendar isn't in front of him). The move that would actually reduce no-show risk is the button-up protocol: lock the time, state the one-hour block, get the verbal handshake. Brandon identified the problem and then didn't solve it.
coaching_implication: This is a coachable instinct moment. Brandon saw the gap but reached for a social nudge instead of a structural anchor. Manager should replay this line and ask: 'You knew he might forget — what's the tool we have for that?' Walk him to the button-up checklist.

quote: Well, I usually get off work at, like, four, but then I go to the gym after, which is why, yesterday, I said 06:30.
stewart_read: Sean is explaining his schedule in detail — gym after work, 6:30 as the window. This is the natural moment to deploy the no-driving/at-computer anchor from the button-up protocol. Sean is telling Brandon exactly where he'll be and what he'll be doing at appointment time. Brandon does not use this to confirm 'you'll be home, off the gym floor, able to sit at a computer.' The information is present; the anchor is not made.
coaching_implication: The customer handed Brandon the scheduling context. Manager should ask: 'He told you he'd be done with the gym by 6:30 — what should you have confirmed about where he'd be and what he'd need for the appointment?' This is the no-driving/at-computer anchor applied to a real customer moment, not an abstract protocol.

quote: I don't know what the fuck I'm doing. Oh, I'm looking at a haircut.
stewart_read: Sean's aside — distracted, checking his schedule, unsure of his own calendar — is a soft re-qualification signal. He is not fully present in this conversation. A rep reading this correctly would recognize that the appointment needs a stronger anchor, not a lighter one. Brandon does not adjust. The call continues toward a close without any escalation of the confirmation structure despite Sean's demonstrated distraction.
coaching_implication: This moment is worth replaying in the 1-on-1. Ask Brandon: 'What did Sean's behavior in this moment tell you about how solid this appointment was?' The goal is to build Brandon's read on customer reliability signals so he knows when to escalate the confirmation structure.

quote: Yeah. I can do that. We'll go ahead and text you, just to confirm with you if Monday at 06:30 still works. If it does, great. Just call me again, and we'll see you then.
stewart_read: The confirmation structure is passive and puts the burden back on Sean. 'If it still works, call me again' is the opposite of a locked appointment — it re-opens the door for Sean to simply not call. The button-up protocol requires: appointment datetime confirmed, one-hour block stated, phone appointment specified, no-driving/at-computer anchor, verbal handshake ('Is that fair?'), and text follow-up promise. Brandon delivers only the text follow-up promise, and even that is framed as a Monday-of reminder rather than an immediate confirmation.
coaching_implication: Walk Brandon through the button_up checklist item by item against this call. Five of six required elements are missing. The manager should frame this as a paycheck-protection issue: a sit that no-shows doesn't pay, and Sean has already no-showed once. The button-up protocol is the tool that changes that.

quote: Yeah. Yeah. Text me again. Text me on Monday. Just so yeah. I don't forget because I'll be forgetting shit.
stewart_read: Sean closes the call by explicitly flagging his own unreliability a second time. This is the clearest possible signal that the appointment is fragile. Brandon's response — 'Alright. Sounds good. Thanks.' — is a clean close socially but a missed opportunity structurally. The verbal handshake ('Is that fair?') is the one move that would have converted Sean's passive agreement into an active commitment. It was not deployed.
coaching_implication: This is the anchor moment for the entire coaching conversation. Sean told Brandon twice he forgets things. The verbal handshake exists for exactly this customer. Manager should ask Brandon to say the handshake line out loud in the 1-on-1 and then ask: 'Why didn't you use it here?' Build the habit of deploying the handshake when the customer signals low reliability — not despite it.
